techniques.evidence_quote: The initial attack vector of these AWS account compromises was the zero-day SugarCRM vulnerability, CVE-2023-22952... This vulnerability allows threat actors to inject custom PHP code through the SugarCRM email templates module due to missing input validation.

techniques.technique_name: Command and Scripting Interpreter: Unix Shell
techniques.evidence_quote: CVE-2023-22952 was published by NIST National Vulnerability Database on Jan. 11, 2023... This vulnerability allows threat actors to inject custom PHP code through the SugarCRM email templates module due to missing input validation.
the threat actors proceeded to create new EC2 instances... ImportKeyPair command to import their public key pair that they created with a third-party tool.
the threat actors did not attempt to create new IAM users like we have seen in other cases. They instead opted for attempting to login as the Root user.

The threat actors created EC2 instances in the same regions that the organizations used for the rest of their normal infrastructure. They also switched regions to a geographically new area... and created a new security group that allowed port 22 SSH traffic from any IP address.

stopping these resources also avoids incurring extra costs in the organization's account... threat actors stopping the resources they created minimizes costs and helps prevent triggering a cost alert.

Once the threat actors gained access to the EC2 instances, they successfully compromised long-term AWS access keys that existed on the host... these plain-text credentials existed on the compromised hosts, which allowed the threat actors to steal them.

They utilized the tools Pacu and Scout Suite to gain a better understanding of what resources existed within the AWS account... these tools scanned a variety of traditional services such as EC2, IAM, RDS, S3, SNS, SES, Lambda.
Before the threat actors performed any scanning activity, they first ran the command GetCallerIdentity. GetCallerIdentity is the AWS version of whoami.

The threat actors also performed other discovery calls against services that would not necessarily be expected that provided helpful information to the attackers, such as the Organizations service and Cost and Usage service.

the threat actors took advantage of misconfigurations to expand their access after initial compromise... allowed the threat actors to steal them and start using the access keys for discovery activity.

the attackers modified pre-existing security group rules... creating brand new databases from the snapshots, making them public and attaching the modified security groups. Finally, they modified the newly created RDS databases by changing the master user password, which would allow them to log in to the databases.

attack_chain.description: Threat actors exploited CVE-2023-22952, a zero-day authentication bypass and RCE vulnerability in SugarCRM, by injecting custom PHP code via the email templates module due to missing input validation, gaining direct access to the underlying EC2 instances.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: This vulnerability allows threat actors to inject custom PHP code through the SugarCRM email templates module due to missing input validation.

attack_chain.description: After gaining a foothold on the EC2 instances, attackers read plaintext long-term AWS access keys stored in the AWS CLI credentials file on the compromised hosts.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: these plain-text credentials existed on the compromised hosts, which allowed the threat actors to steal them and start using the access keys for discovery activity.

attack_chain.description: Using the stolen access keys, attackers called GetCallerIdentity (the AWS equivalent of 'whoami') to enumerate the identity, account ID, and ARN associated with the compromised credentials.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: Before the threat actors performed any scanning activity, they first ran the command GetCallerIdentity. GetCallerIdentity is the AWS version of whoami.

attack_chain.description: Attackers used Pacu (an open-source AWS exploitation framework) and Scout Suite (a cloud security auditing tool) to enumerate AWS resources across EC2, IAM, RDS, S3, SNS, SES, and Lambda.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: They utilized the tools Pacu and Scout Suite to gain a better understanding of what resources existed within the AWS account... these tools scanned a variety of traditional services such as EC2, IAM, RDS, S3, SNS, SES, Lambda.

attack_chain.description: Attackers made Organizations API calls (ListOrganizationalUnitsForParent, ListAccounts, DescribeOrganization) to enumerate the master account ID and email address, and used GetCostAndUsage to determine account spending/activity levels.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: three Organizations API calls stood out... DescribeOrganization API call. This event returns the master account ID as well as the master account email address associated with that account. With that information, the threat actors have enough to attempt to log in as the Root user.

attack_chain.technique_name: Modify Cloud Compute Infrastructure: Create Snapshot
attack_chain.description: Attackers moved laterally to the RDS service and created new RDS snapshots from the SugarCRM RDS databases without causing downtime to the original databases.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The threat actors moved laterally from the compromised SugarCRM EC2 instances to the RDS service and started executing commands to create new RDS snapshots from the various SugarCRM RDS databases.

attack_chain.technique_name: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Cloud Firewall
attack_chain.description: Attackers modified pre-existing security group rules to add port 3306 (MySQL) to inbound rules, enabling external access to newly created RDS databases.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the attackers modified pre-existing security group rules that already allowed SSH inbound, and they added port 3306 for MySQL traffic.

attack_chain.description: Attackers created new public-facing RDS databases from the snapshots, attached the modified security groups allowing MySQL access, and changed the master user password to enable login and data exfiltration.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: creating brand new databases from the snapshots, making them public and attaching the modified security groups. Finally, they modified the newly created RDS databases by changing the master user password, which would allow them to log in to the databases.

attack_chain.technique_name: Modify Cloud Compute Infrastructure: Create Cloud Instance
attack_chain.description: Attackers created AMIs from the running SugarCRM EC2 instances, imported their own SSH key pairs, and launched new EC2 instances in both existing and new/non-standard regions, attaching security groups allowing port 22 from any IP.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the threat actors did was create new Amazon Machine Images (AMIs) from the running SugarCRM EC2 instances... proceeded to create new EC2 instances... also attached existing security groups to the EC2 instances that allowed inbound port 22 access from any IP address.

attack_chain.description: Using master account email and ID obtained via Organizations API calls, attackers attempted to log in as the AWS Root user to escalate privileges. The attempts failed and were visible in CloudTrail logs.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: Despite using information they obtained from the Organizations calls made during the discovery phase, the threat actors still failed to successfully log in as the Root user.

attack_chain.technique_name: Modify Cloud Compute Infrastructure: Create Cloud Instance
attack_chain.description: For defense evasion, attackers deployed EC2 instances in non-standard/unused regions and repeatedly started and stopped instances to reduce cost footprint and avoid detection in the default AWS console view (which shows only running instances).
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the threat actors deployed resources in non-standard regions... they also turned the EC2 instances on and off throughout their time in the environments... stopping these resources also avoids incurring extra costs.
